coredata.dc:title: Ce-UiO-66-F4-based composites decorated with green carbon dots for universal adsorption of organic pollutants containing hydrogen bond donors and its application exploration
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Maozhen
Yu, Hengjie
He, Yingchao
Xu, Weidong
Liu, Da
Cheng, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Efficient and stable water purification is essential for the protection of water resources. Current adsorbents based on metal–organic frameworks face limitations of restricted active sites and single adsorption objects. In this study, we developed a cost-effective and environmentally friendly Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites by growing Tea and L-tryptophan carbon dots (Tea-L-CDs) over Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF, and the interaction force was enhanced by hydrogen bonding (-F/O/N…H-O-, -F/O/N…H-N-, etc.) to achieve universal adsorption. In detail, the maximum adsorption capacity (qm) of the adsorbent for trypan blue (TB), congo red (CR), and CR (adjust pH) was 773.4, 1428.6, and 4761.9 mg/g. The universal experiment showed that the composites exhibited excellent qm for alizarin red S (ARS, 662.7 mg/g) and rhodamine B (RdB, 374.0 mg/g). Meanwhile, Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites maintained good anti-interference ability (salt solution, temperature, etc.). Remarkably, the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent for dyes exceeded 97 % of qm within 1 min of contact time. The adsorption performance of synthetic material for TB, CR, ARS, and RdB is better than most of the adsorbents, especially the qm for TB is higher than any adsorbent previously reported. Besides, this adsorbent had excellent storage stability in the air for 30 days. Based on this nanomaterial, we successfully developed an automated dye filtration and contamination assessment system for blocking dye-attached adsorbent and online detection of dye residues after water purification. Thus, the synthesized Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites, with universal adsorption, high efficiency, and good storage stability, hold great potential for wastewater purification.

head.ce:title: Ce-UiO-66-F4-based composites decorated with green carbon dots for universal adsorption of organic pollutants containing hydrogen bond donors and its application exploration

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: 866 Yuhangtang Rd., Hangzhou 310058, Zhejiang, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient and stable water purification is essential for the protection of water resources. Current adsorbents based on metal–organic frameworks face limitations of restricted active sites and single adsorption objects. In this study, we developed a cost-effective and environmentally friendly Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites by growing Tea and L-tryptophan carbon dots (Tea-L-CDs) over Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF, and the interaction force was enhanced by hydrogen bonding (-F/O/N…H-O-, -F/O/N…H-N-, etc.) to achieve universal adsorption. In detail, the maximum adsorption capacity (qm) of the adsorbent for trypan blue (TB), congo red (CR), and CR (adjust pH) was 773.4, 1428.6, and 4761.9 mg/g. The universal experiment showed that the composites exhibited excellent qm for alizarin red S (ARS, 662.7 mg/g) and rhodamine B (RdB, 374.0 mg/g). Meanwhile, Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites maintained good anti-interference ability (salt solution, temperature, etc.). Remarkably, the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent for dyes exceeded 97 % of qm within 1 min of contact time. The adsorption performance of synthetic material for TB, CR, ARS, and RdB is better than most of the adsorbents, especially the qm for TB is higher than any adsorbent previously reported. Besides, this adsorbent had excellent storage stability in the air for 30 days. Based on this nanomaterial, we successfully developed an automated dye filtration and contamination assessment system for blocking dye-attached adsorbent and online detection of dye residues after water purification. Thus, the synthesized Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites, with universal adsorption, high efficiency, and good storage stability, hold great potential for wastewater purification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic dyes are extensively utilized in fields such as textile coloring, cell staining, biological disease diagnosis, and element determination, serving the needs of industrial and emerging technology development [1,2]. The annual output of dyes is more than five million tons and has a huge consumption [3]. Besides, the WGK Germany (water hazard class) of some dyes is 3, such as trypan blue (TB), congo red (CR), alizarin red S (ARS), and rhodamine B (RdB) [4–6], indicating that dyes are hazardous to water bodies. Untreated wastewater may be discharged into the ecosystem during actual production processes. Due to the excellent water solubility of certain dyes, even small quantities of dyes can pose a threat to water bodies, animals, or plants through the cumulative effects of toxicity, ultimately endangering human health [7]. More seriously, TB and RdB, classified by the International Agency for Research on Cancer as Group 2B and Group 3 carcinogens, will further pollute already scarce water resources and diminish the availability of high-quality water sources [8]. Therefore, one of the critical measures to ensure water resource security is to improve the treatment efficiency of organic dyes in wastewater and the residual detection speed after water purification.

Recently, many methods have been employed to develop the composites for removing dyes from wastewater. Commonly used methods include biological treatment [9], condensation precipitation [10], photocatalytic degradation [11], electrochemical degradation [12], and physical or chemical adsorption [3,13]. In practical applications, the biological treatment method is used to purify wastewater due to its low operating costs [14]. However, the wastewater treatment cycle is relatively long, and the degradation effect of macromolecular substances is relatively low. Thus, this method is often combined with condensation precipitation to improve the wastewater treatment efficiency [15]. But it further increases the wastewater treatment cycle. Compared with the degradation method, the adsorption method has the advantages of rapidity, retaining the structure of dyes, and reusability [16]. Nonetheless, the cost of the adsorption method presently surpasses that of conventional wastewater treatment approaches owing to the limited active sites and the narrow range of adsorbable substances [17]. Many adsorbents exhibit excellent adsorption performance for individual dyes, but the lack of good removal ability for multi-dyes limits application [18–20].

As a new type of nanomaterial, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have been used as potential adsorbents for wastewater purification due to their porosity and high specific surface area [21,22]. Many MOFs have been designed and demonstrated excellent adsorption performance for organic pollutants, such as ZIF series, MIL series, and UiO series [23–25]. Co-MOF, derived from ZIF-67, efficiently removed CR from wastewater and exhibited superior recovery performance compared to ZIF-67 [26]. Upon mixing with polymeric beads, ZIF-8 was strengthened in its π-π stacking and electrostatic attraction with dye molecules, achieving more efficient wastewater remediation [27]. G-MIL-101(Cr)@R, synthesized through an environmentally friendly method, successfully removed acid blue 92 dye from wastewater [28]. Organic pollutants can be adsorbed into the pores and onto the surfaces of MOF-5 and MOF-199, highlighting the applicability of MOFs in wastewater treatment [29,30]. Besides, UiO-66 has been extensively studied to explore the potential for engineering applications because of its excellent hydrothermal and chemical stability [4,31,32]. UiO-66-based composites have been proved to be effective in treating antibiotics or organic dyes in water, and pollutant-adsorbed UiO-66 can be easily removed from wastewater under hydrophobic action [33,34]. Since the synthesis of UiO-66 based on the Zr metal center, the adsorption capacity of Zr-UiO-66 MOF for organic compounds has been continuously improved after physical or chemical modification [32,35]. The polydopamine-modified Zr-UiO-66 exhibited super hydrophilicity, and demonstrated great potential for efficient purification of wastewater after embedding graphene oxide [36]. Zr-UiO-66-NH2 MOF, functionalized with polyethyleneimine, showed excellent adsorption capacity for methyl orange and heavy metal ions within 5 min [37]. Recently, Ce-based UiO-66 has been efficiently synthesized with lower energy consumption (100 ℃, 15 min), showing higher cost-effectiveness than Zr-UiO-66 MOF [5,38]. The hydrogen bonds formed between hydrogen bond donors and acceptors are one of the common adsorption mechanisms of Ce-UiO-66 MOF. Hydrogen bond donors are present in a large number of dyes, such as –OH and –NH2 groups in TB (Table S1). Hence, increasing the number of hydrogen bond acceptors in Ce-UiO-66 MOF is a feasible choice for achieving efficient and universal adsorption.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a type of small-sized (1 ∼ 10 nm) fluorescent nanomaterials, are widely employed in multiple fields [39,40]. Because of low biological toxicity, CDs are often studied as alternatives for quantum dots to reduce the environmental impact of heavy metals [41]. CDs have abundant hydrogen bond donors/acceptors on their surface, which can be modified for different research purposes [42]. Biomass CDs, as a common type of CDs, have the advantages of safety, non-toxicity, simple synthesis, and low cost [43]. Although abundant oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups can form hydrogen bonds with dye molecules, the excellent water solubility of biomass CDs makes it difficult to remove dyes from wastewater [44]. Recently, biomass CDs were adsorbed on the surface of chitosan through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attraction to synthesize a gel film with good performance [45]. However, due to the abundant hydrophilic functional groups of biomass CDs, some biomass CDs may fall off from the composites and enter the water during use. Thus, the synthesis of environmentally friendly and cost-effective biomass CDs contributes to the development of an economically efficient adsorbent.

Inspired by the strong electronegativity of fluorine and the rich functional groups of CDs, this work developed a cost-effective and environmentally friendly Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 adsorbent by growing Tea and L-tryptophan CDs (Tea-L-CDs) over Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF. Using Assam tea and L-tryptophan as raw materials, green and low-cost Tea-L-CDs were extracted via a hydrothermal method. Furthermore, Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF was successfully synthesized with 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalic acid as a precursor. Specifically, (1) the composites were characterized by ten techniques to understand their physical and chemical properties, including field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), atomic force microscope (AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA), Brunauer Emmett and Teller analysis (BET), and zeta potential analysis; (2) the adsorption kinetics, adsorption isotherm, and adsorption thermodynamics of this adsorbent for TB and CR were investigated. Additionally, the influencing factors that may be encountered in practical applications were explored, including pH, dye concentration, adsorbent dosage, temperature, salt concentration, mixed dyes, real-time water samples, storage time, packaging materials, adsorption–desorption, etc.; (3) the universality of adsorption was explored using ARS and RdB, which contained hydrogen bond donors; (4) the adsorption mechanism of the adsorbent was analyzed through various characterization methods, and further investigation was conducted into other factors influencing the adsorption mechanisms; (5) a filtration device was designed and demonstrated to have rapid water purification capabilities after adsorption. Based on this filtration device and the nanomaterials, an automated dye filtration and contamination assessment system was developed for blocking dye-attached composites and online detection of residual dye after wastewater purification.

Cerium ammonium nitrate (NH4)2Ce(NO3)6 (≥99 %) and 2,3,5,6-Tetrafluoroterephthalic acid (H2BDC-F4, ≥ 97 %) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Assam tea was purchased from Xisili Tea Factory. L-Tryptophan (99 %) was obtained from Meryer Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Organic dyes were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., including TB, CR, RdB, ARS, Methylene blue (MB), Methyl Orange (MO). All chemicals do not need further purification.

0.5 g of Assam tea and 60 mg of L-tryptophan were added to a Teflon flask (Scheme 1 a). After adding 10 mL of ultrapure water, the flask was sonicated for 10 min and then moved to an autoclave. Next, the autoclave was heated at 200 ℃ for 12 h. When the Teflon flask was naturally cooled, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to obtain dispersion 1. Subsequently, dispersion 1 was filtered by a 0.22 um filter membrane to obtain dispersion 2. Finally, brown Tea-L-CDs solution was obtained after dialysis of dispersion 2 and stored in a refrigerator (4 °C) before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ce-UiO-66-F4 and Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites
ce:section.ce:para: Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites were synthesized in a Teflon-lined autoclave by the synthesis method of Ce-UiO-66 MOF as a reference [46]. Briefly, 1.28 mmol of H2BDC-F4 was placed in a glass beaker. Then, 7.2 mL of N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was added under constant stirring. When H2BDC-F4 was completely dissolved, (NH4)2Ce(NO3)6 (2.4 mL, 0.53 mol/L) was added drop by drop to the glass beaker. After the initial reaction completed, the mixture was moved to a Teflon-lined autoclave at 100 ℃ for 15 min. The precipitate was collected after the solution cooled to room temperature and was centrifuged. After several times of mother liquor washing, the reactant was washed twice with DMF to remove unreacted linkers, and subsequently washed four times with acetone for solvent displacement. The dried Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF was obtained from a vacuum oven (80 ℃, 24 h) and then stored at 4 ℃ (nitrogen atmosphere).

Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites were prepared by continuous attachment of Tea-L-CDs nanoparticles onto MOF (Scheme 1a). Compared to the synthesis process of Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF, the H2BDC-F4 solution was homogeneously mixed with 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1 mL of Tea-L-CDs dispersion and labeled as Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-x (x = 1, 2, 3, 4), respectively. Other than that, the rest of the process was the same for all synthesized materials.
The details of adsorption experiments and characterization can be obtained in supplementary information (Sections S2&S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of an automated dye filtration and contamination assessment system
Assessment of dye residue levels after wastewater purification based on deep convolutional neural networks (DCNNs)
ce:section.ce:para: Deep learning algorithms are widely used for object detection and pattern recognition [47]. Compared with traditional machine learning models, DCNNs have better robustness for solving complex classification problems [48]. Thus, this work designed and fabricated an automated dye residue detection device based on DCNNs and optical principles, taking TB and CR as examples.

Firstly, light-emitting diodes with different wavelengths were used as light sources for TB (570, 580, and 595 nm) and CR (500, 540, and 570 nm) concentrations detection according to the peaks of ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra in Table S1. The camera was used to receive light transmitted from an aqueous solution containing TB or CR under the illumination of different light sources. Subsequently, 1158 images were captured to record data of transmitted light at different TB and CR concentrations, including ultrapure water, pure water for drinking, and tap water as solvents. Then, four lightweight models were selected from the DCNNs to be used for the training of the dataset (Scheme 1b), containing MobileNetV2 [49], MobileNetV3 [50], Mobileone [51], and SqueezeNetV2 [52]. The dataset was divided into a training set and a validation set according to the ratio of 3:1. Finally, the four trained lightweight models were deployed to the automated dye residue detection device, and the stability of the model was verified by 30 images of TB and CR captured in this device online.

ce:section.ce:section-title: User interface (UI) and control system design
ce:section.ce:para: The control system was mainly composed of an upper computer and a lower computer. The control commands were channeled through the Arduino microcontroller (lower computer), which was mainly used for simple control tasks in this work, such as the on–off of the drainage valve. Compared to the lower computer, data processing, model application, and online detection can be realized by the upper computer. Human-computer interaction can be easily implemented through the UI. The specific control flow was shown in Scheme 1c. In detail, the dye contamination assessment model was deployed in a cloud server. Through the communication between the cloud server and the local computer, the local models were updated for online detection. When adsorption was completed, valve 1 was automatically opened and closed. Then, the contamination level of TB or CR was assessed online by the DCNNs. After the evaluation was completed, valve 2 was opened immediately, and valves 3 ∼ 5 were automatically opened according to the classification results displayed in the UI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the optimal Tea-L-CDs volume
ce:section.ce:para: To preliminarily evaluate the adsorption performance of the composites, 10 mg of adsorbent was added to separate solution of 20 mL containing 250 mg/L of TB or 400 mg/L of CR, followed by stirring for 10 min. From Fig. 1 a, Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-1 composites had higher removal efficiency of TB and CR than Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF, which proved that Tea-L-CDs can promote the adsorption of TB and CR. Subsequently, adsorption experiments were conducted using adsorbents synthesized from 0.25 to 1 mL of Tea-L-CDs solution to determine the optimal volume. The concentration of TB changed from 250 to 400 mg/L. As shown in Fig. 1b, the removal efficiency of TB and CR first increased and then decreased with the increase of Tea-L-CDs dosage. Compared with other synthesized materials, Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites exhibited the best adsorption performance, with the highest removal rates for TB and CR reaching 90.4 % and 96.6 %, respectively.

The surface morphology of the adsorbents and Tea-L-CDs were analyzed by FESEM images. Fig. 2 a shows the cubic-shaped crystal of Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF, where it can be seen that the addition of elemental fluorine has slight effect on the surface morphology of Ce-UiO-66 MOF [4,38]. From Fig. 2b–e, it can be inferred that the surface morphology of the obtained composites gradually becomes a cuboid-shaped crystal with the increase of Tea-L-CDs. The cuboid-shaped crystal did not change significantly when the appropriate volume (0 ∼ 0.5 mL) of Tea-L-CDs was used to synthesize the adsorbent, preliminarily explaining the trend of adsorption performance in Section 3.1. Fig. 2f depicts the surface morphology of Tea-L-CDs that have significantly smaller particle sizes than Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF. Tea-L-CDs were aggregated into larger spheres due to their hydrophilic nature and had more and stronger absorption peaks at 200 ∼ 300 nm than Tea-CDs recorded by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Fig. S1a). Meanwhile, blue fluorescence was reflected from the Tea-L-CDs under UV light (365 nm) excitation compared to pure water and tea (Fig. S1c and d), confirming that Tea-L-CDs have rich functional groups. Although Tea-L-CDs possess abundant functional groups, an excessive amount of Tea-L-CDs may occupy too many adsorption sites, potentially leading to a decrease in the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent. Next, the elemental composition of the three adsorbents was determined by EDX and shown in Fig. S2. The proportions of nitrogen in Ce-UiO-66-F4, Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2, and Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-4 composites were increased sequentially, exhibiting the effect of Tea-L-CDs on the elemental composition of the adsorbents. Besides, Fig. 2g displays the elemental mapping of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites, showing the presence and uniform distribution of cerium (Ce), carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and fluorine (F) elements. Thus, FESEM analysis and EDX analysis prove the successful synthesis of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites.

After being dispersed in water and dried, the three-dimensional structure and height change of the synthetic materials were obtained and shown in Fig. 2h–k and S3. Tea-L-CDs exhibited heights ranging from 1 to 16 nm, forming two circular regions with diameters of 50 to 100 nm, which may be attributed to the stacking of Tea-L-CDs. The low height indicated that Tea-L-CDs have smaller particle sizes, consistent with previous research findings [45]. Particle stacking also appeared in the AFM images of the three adsorbents. However, flake stacking was gradually recorded in the three-dimensional images with the increase of Tea-L-CDs, and it was similar to the trend of cuboid-shaped crystals scanned in FESEM.

XPS was used to determine the element content, functional groups, and atomic valence states to further analyze the effect of Tea-L-CDs volumes on Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF. From Fig. S4a, it can be seen that Tea-L-CDs have four elemental peaks corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s, and S2p. Additionally, the peak of F1s appeared in the XPS spectra of three adsorbents compared with Ce-UiO-66 MOF [5], and the analyzer began to receive the signal of S2p when the volume of Tea-L-CDs increased to 1 mL (Fig. 3 a and S5a and S6a). The high-resolution C1s of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2/4 composites was divided into four peaks at 284.8 eV (CC/CC), 286.2 eV (CO/CN), 287.2 eV (CO/CF), and 288.5 eV (OCO) from Fig. 3b and S6b, which were similar to the binding energy of the remaining adsorbents [43,53–55]. CF and CN bonds almost did not exist in the C1s high-resolution spectra of Tea-L-CDs and Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF, respectively (Fig. S4b and S5b). The peak of N1s was clearly visible in the XPS spectra of Tea-L-CDs and Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2/4 composites (Fig. 3c and S4c andS6c). The binding energy of 400 eV was recorded as the CNH bond except for Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF [36], and the signal of the CNC bond was detected by the analyzer with a binding energy of 401.5 eV for Tea-L-CDs and Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-4 composites [43]. From Fig. 3d and S5c and S6d, the high-resolution O1s of three adsorbents exhibited three peaks with a binding energy of 530.1 eV, 531.4 eV, and 532.3 eV, which are related to CeO, OH, and OCO bonds, respectively [5,56]. The peaks of high-resolution O1S were shifted to the higher binding energy in the spectrum of Tea-L-CDs, corresponding to the binding energy of 531.4 eV, 532.3 eV, and 533.4 eV (CO) (Fig. S4d) [57]. The same peak of high-resolution F1s was observed from the spectrum of Ce-UiO-66-F4 and Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2/4 composites (Fig. 3e and S5d and S6e), with a binding energy of 686.7 eV (C-F) [55]. Ce metal in the oxidation states of Ce3+ and Ce4+ was observed from the deconvolutional Ce3d high-resolution spectra [56]. The peaks of Ce3+ were scanned at 882.9 eV, 885.2 eV, 887.7 eV, and 898.5 eV, and the peaks at 901.5 eV, 906 eV, and 916.9 eV represent Ce4+ (Fig. S5e) [5]. The peak of Ce3+ at 887.7 eV disappeared with the addition of Tea-L-CDs (Fig. 3f), which may be attributed to the chemical adsorption between Tea-L-CDs and Ce3+. According to the element content in Table S2 and the above analysis, the adsorbent with strong electronegative fluorine and rich functional groups was successfully synthesized through the growth of Tea-L-CDs over Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF.

From Fig. 3g, the pattern of Tea-L-CDs was in good agreement with previously reported Tea-CDs in wavelengths ranging from 500 to 1200 cm−1 [45], and the broad peak shifted from 3418 to 3223 cm−1 under the influence of N-H stretching vibration [37], revealing the successful extraction of Tea-L-CDs. FTIR spectra of the three adsorbents had a high similarity, which is well matched with the absorption peak of Ce-UiO-66 MOF [5]. In detail, the adsorbents' peaks in the 480 ∼ 800 cm−1 range were mainly related to the vibration of CeO bond [58], which is formed by the interaction between Ce3+ and some ligands. The absorption peaks at 995 and 1267 cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibration and the stretching vibration of the CF bond [59], providing a theoretical basis for fluorine-containing hydrogen bonds. The peak for the N-H bending in the Tea-L-CDs was recorded at 1520 cm−1, and this absorption peak of the adsorbents gradually formed as the Tea-L-CDs increased. Meanwhile, the peak of the adsorbents shifted from 1605 to 1598 cm−1 under the influence of Tea-L-CDs. It is worth noting that the peak around 1650 cm−1 gradually shrunk when the volume of Tea-L-CDs increased to 1 mL, mainly because the pores of Ce-MOF are occupied by Tea-L-CDs, resulting in a decrease in acetone [5]. The broad peak at 3150 ∼ 3600 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of the O-H bond and N-H, changing from 3422 to 3405 cm−1 under the influence of Tea-L-CDs (N-H stretching), which is consistent with the variation between Tea-CDs [45] and Tea-L-CDs (This work). Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites with abundant functional groups were successfully synthesized through the interaction between Tea-L-CDs and Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF.

XRD was performed on Ce-UiO-66-F4 and Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites to analyze the change of crystal planes. From Fig. 3h, it can be seen that most diffraction peaks are consistent with the standard simulation diagram of NO.1036904 (Ce-UiO-66) from Cambridge Crystallographic Data Center, indicating that Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites still have a similar crystal structure to Ce-UiO-66 MOF. However, some diffraction peaks shifted or disappeared with the increase of -F groups and Tea-L-CDs. Compared with Ce-UiO-66 MOF, the diffraction peak of Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF became wider at 2θ = 8.2° (002) and disappeared at 2θ = 35.4° (066). With the increase of Tea-L-CDs, the diffraction peak at 2θ = 8.2° (002) shifted to 2θ = 8.4° (002), and the diffraction peaks tended to disappear at 2θ = 11.6° (022), 14.3° (222), 20.2° (224), and 33.3° (008). Therefore, Tea-L-CDs were confirmed to be involved in the synthesis of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites.

Fig. 3i shows the N2 adsorption/degradation curve of the adsorbents to explore the effect of surface characteristics on the adsorption capacity. Compared with Ce-UiO-66 MOF, Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF had a smaller specific surface area (149.5 m2/g) and larger average pore size (14.36 nm) [4], revealing the significant influence of the -F group on the synthetic material. With the increase of Tea-L-CDs, the specific surface area (128.2 ∼ 124.9 m2/g) and pore volume (0.418 ∼ 0.398 cm3/g) gradually decreased (Table 1 ), while the pore size gradually increased (17.2 ∼ 17.93 nm). The pore size of a material is influenced by various factors, but the main reason for significant changes in pore size is the length and quantity of organic functional group ligands [59,60]. Compared to Ce-UiO-66 MOF, Ce-UiO-66-F MOF had a larger pore size and a smaller specific surface area (Fig. S7). However, Ce-UiO-66-F MOF had smaller pore size and larger specific surface area compared with Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF, indicating that the organic functional ligands have a significant impact on the pore size. Tea-L-CDs contained abundant organic functional group ligands, which may further lead to an increase in the pore size of the Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF. Three adsorbents have the characteristics of type II isotherm in the adsorption/desorption process, suggesting that the material has the potential for multi-layer adsorption. Excessive Tea-L-CDs may reduce the specific surface area of Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF and occupy many active sites, such as the -F group. Combining the above results (FESEM and AFM), it can be used to explain why the adsorption capacity of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-3/4 composites decreased (Section 3.1).

Fig. 3j depicts the TGA curves of Ce-UiO-66-F4 and Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2/4 composites, which can be used to understand the thermal stability of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites. The designed adsorbents had similar TGA curves, especially when the temperature exceeded 200 °C. The first step of weight loss was observed between 30 and 200 °C, caused by the removal of water, acetone, or DMF molecules [4]. Subsequently, the sharp weight loss of about 34 % occurred in the range of 200 ∼ 400 °C due to the collapse of MOF or the decomposition of the organic linkers [62]. The last stage of weight loss (About 10 %) in the 400 ∼ 800 °C range may be caused by the decomposition of the Ce-UiO-66-F4 framework. The weight losses of Ce-UiO-66-F4, Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2, and Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-4 composites were 50.4 %, 50.1 % and 49.6 % at 800 °C, respectively. In general, Tea-L-CDs had no significant effect on the thermal stability of Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF.

The adsorption performance may be influenced by various factors such as pH, salt concentrations. Therefore, the factors that may impact the efficiency of wastewater purification were studied and discussed in the below sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of contact time and adsorption kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: The contact time between the adsorbent and dyes is significant for engineering applications. The optimal contact time was determined using TB solution (20 mL, 275 mg/L), CR solution (20 mL, 350 mg/L), and the adsorbent (10 mg each time). It is worth noting that 94.5 % of TB molecules and 95.6 % of CR molecules were adsorbed onto the surface or pores of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites within 1 min of contact time (Fig. 4 a). When the time interval increased from 1 to 60 min, the removal efficiency of dyes only increased by 2 ∼ 3 %, confirming that the majority of adsorption sites reached saturation within a short period. The adsorbent may not be fully in contact with the dye within 1 min, especially in large-scale wastewater treatment processes. Thus, the 5 ∼ 10 min interval can be considered as the optimal contact time for this adsorbent in applications. A time interval of 10 min was chosen as the contact time between the adsorbent and the dye in the following experiments to fully understand the adsorption potential of the composites.

The adsorption kinetic models were used to explore the adsorption mechanism and the rate-limiting step. Fig. 5 a–c show the linear fitting of the three kinetic models, and the kinetic parameters were provided in Table S3. The qe,cal of TB and CR in pseudo-second-order kinetic were 526.3 mg/g and 666.7 mg/g, respectively, which were close to the experimental qe,exp of TB (531.4 mg/g) and CR (680.3 mg/g). The R2 of the pseudo-second-order kinetic (Value: 1.0) was significantly higher than the R2 of the pseudo-first-order kinetic and the Weber-Morris intra-particle diffusion models, revealing that the adsorption rate of the dye was mainly dominated by chemical adsorption. Besides, the constant C of Weber-Morris model was not 0, indicating that the adsorption process is affected by various adsorption types.

Owing to the presence of H+ and OH−, the adsorption capacity may be affected by pH [5]. The performance of the adsorbent (10 mg) was investigated in TB solution (20 mL, 350 mg/L) and CR solution (20 mL, 500 mg/L) at six different pH. From Fig. 4b, it can be observed that the adsorption capacity of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites for TB was less affected by pH under acidic and weakly alkaline conditions. However, the removal efficiencies for TB were about 79.8 % and 15.2 % at pH 10 and 12, respectively, revealing the deactivation of most adsorption sites. Compared with TB, CR was more difficult to be adsorbed under alkaline conditions, especially at pH 12. Remarkably, the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent for CR was significantly improved with the decrease in pH. Fig. S8a–d showed that the Zeta potential of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites changed from 16.7 to − 16 mV as the pH increased from 2 to 12, and was 7.7 mV in ultrapure water. The variation in surface potential of the adsorbent reflected that the electrostatic attraction mechanism is significantly influenced by pH. Besides, the crystal structure of UiO-66 MOF would be disrupted under high pH (strong alkaline conditions), leading to the weakening of hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and the pore filling (Section S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of dye concentration and adsorbent dosage
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of TB increased from 100 to 500 mg/L, and the concentration of CR changed from 200 to 1000 mg/L. From Fig. 4c, it can be observed that the removal efficiency of the dyes was not significantly affected by concentration changes when the adsorbent was increased in the same proportion, confirming the excellent adsorption stability of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites. Next, 10 ∼ 30 mg of adsorbent was added into TB solution (20 mL, 100 mg/L) and CR solution (20 mL, 200 mg/L) to investigate the effect of adsorbent dosage on dye removal efficiency. As shown in Fig. 4d, when consuming 30 mg of adsorbent, only about 1.5 % of the removal efficiency was improved compared to using 10 mg of composites. Therefore, the suitable dosage of this synthetic material can be selected based on specific requirements for dye removal.

In practical applications, the ambient temperature is not constant. To understand the influence of temperature, dye adsorption experiments were conducted on TB solution (20 mL, 300 mg/L) and CR solution (20 mL, 350 mg/L) under water bath heating conditions. From Fig. 4e, the adsorption capacity of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites gradually decreased as the temperature increased from 25 to 70 ℃. At 70 °C, the removal efficiency of TB decreased by about 2 %, while the removal efficiency of CR decreased by about 4 %. Fig. S9 showed the change curve of the Zeta potential of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites from 20 to 50 ℃. At higher temperatures, the Zeta potential of the adsorbent decreased from 9.8 to 6.1 mV, indicating the weakening of the electrostatic attraction on the adsorbent surface. Consequently, during the dye adsorption process, the ambient temperature can be controlled below 50 °C to enhance the adsorption efficiency of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites.

The adsorption capacity of synthetic material may be affected by the electrostatic attraction between the salt ions and the adsorbent. For instance, TB is typically dissolved in salt solutions for cell staining, which contains chloride ions. Different concentrations of NaCl, CH3COONa, and Na2SO4 (10 ∼ 100 mmol/L) were added to TB and CR solutions to investigate the effect of salt concentrations. From Fig. 4f–h, the removal efficiency of TB was negatively correlated with the increase of salt concentration, especially in the solution containing CH3COO−. About 71 % and 62 % of TB molecules were removed in 100 mmol/L Cl− and SO4 2−, respectively. However, in CH3COO− solution, only about 30 % of TB molecules were adsorbed. The lowest removal efficiency of CR was about 80 % in the solution containing SO4 2−, indicating that CR was less affected by salt ions than TB. It is worth noting that the removal efficiency of CR can still maintain more than 95 % in 100 mmol/L CH3COO−. The surface of the adsorbent was positively charged, and salt ions can be adsorbed on the surface of the adsorbent under the influence of electrostatic attraction, which is beneficial for reducing the salt concentration from the sewage. Thus, some measures can be taken to improve the efficiency of dye adsorption, such as reducing the concentration of salt ions or increasing the dosage of this adsorbent.

Adsorption isotherms are crucial for understanding the adsorption mode between the adsorbent and the dye for determining the maximum adsorption capacity (qm) of synthetic material. Due to the significant effect of pH on the removal efficiency of CR in Section 3.3.2, the adsorption isotherms of TB, CR, and CR (pH = 2) were studied and fitted, respectively. From Fig. 5d1 and f1 and e1, it can be found that the R2 value of the Langmuir isotherm was significantly higher than that of the Freundlich and Temkin isotherms during the adsorption of TB, revealing the occurrence of monolayer adsorption. qm of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites for TB was 773.4 mg/g (Table 2 ), which is about 1.6 times that of the adsorbent in the previous study [4]. The Langmuir model exhibited the highest R2 value (0.991) for the adsorption of CR (Fig. 5d2 and e2 and f2), and the calculated qm for CR adsorbed on the adsorbent was 1460 mg/g. After adjusting the pH, the qm of the adsorbent for CR (pH = 2) increased to 4761.9 mg/g. The R2 of the Freundlich model exceeded that of the Langmuir model (Fig. 5d3 and e3 and f3), revealing that the active sites of the adsorbent have multi-layer adsorption under acidic conditions. The Temkin model had a stable R2 in the isotherm study of TB and CR, indicating that there is an electrostatic attraction between the adsorbent and the dyes. According to the linear fitting of the adsorption isotherms, the adsorbent showed excellent adsorption capacity for CR and TB within 10 min.

Adsorption thermodynamics is essential for understanding the thermal properties of design materials, especially from the perspective of energy conversion. In addition to the weakening of electrostatic attraction, higher ambient temperature may affect the formation of hydrogen bonds between the adsorbent and the dye. From Fig. S10, the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent decreased with increasing temperature by analyzing the linear fitting curves of Lnkd and 1/T. As shown in Table S4, the △H of TB and CR became negative during adsorption, revealing that the process is exothermic. The △G proved the spontaneity of TB or CR adsorbed to Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites, and the positive △S represented the stability of chemical adsorption at different temperatures.

The removal of other dyes is important to measure the performance of the adsorbent, which can be used to determine that the designed adsorbent has the potential for practical application. MB (positive charged, no hydrogen bond donor), RdB (positive charged, hydrogen bond donor), MO (negatively charged, no hydrogen bond donor), and ARS (negatively charged, hydrogen bond donor) were used in adsorption experiments to verify the universal adsorption capacity of the adsorbent. The removal efficiencies of MO and ARS were 65.0 % and 97.6 % (Fig. 6 a and b), respectively, indicating that MO is more difficult to be adsorbed on the surface of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites within 10 min. Significantly, the adsorbent showed excellent adsorption performance for cationic dye RhB and achieved a removal efficiency of 95.8 %. However, only 68.0 % removal efficiency was achieved for MB without the hydrogen bond donor (Fig. 6c and d). Through the above analysis, it can be seen that dyes with hydrogen bond donors can be more easily removed from wastewater by Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites, confirming that the adsorbent has the potential to remove various anionic and cationic dyes by strengthening hydrogen bonding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption potential for ARS and RdB
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption capacity of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites for ARS and RdB was studied by adsorption isotherms to understand the application potential of synthetic materials. For the anionic dye ARS, the R2 of the Freundlich isotherm was the highest (0.995), indicating the existence of multi-layer adsorption (Fig. S11a). The monolayer qm of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites for ARS was 662.7 mg/g by fitting the Langmuir isotherm (Table S5). For the cationic dye RdB, the R2 of the Langmuir isotherm was higher than 0.99 and significantly higher than the R2 of the Freundlich and Temkin isotherms (Fig. S11b). qm of the adsorbent for RdB was 374.0 mg/g. The adsorbent showed excellent adsorption capacity for ARS and RdB. Therefore, Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites has good adsorption potential for dyes containing hydrogen bond donors, which helps promote the engineering application of this adsorbent.

Various dyes exist in the wastewater simultaneously, which may affect the adsorption performance of the adsorbent. Hence, two or three dyes were mixed and used to explore the application potential of the adsorbent. TB was mixed with CR, ARS, and RdB to prepare 150 or 100 mg/L mixed dyes, respectively. Similarly, ARS and RdB were added to the CR solution, respectively. 150 mg/L of three mixed dyes containing TB, CR, and ARS were prepared to evaluate the performance of the adsorbent under more complex conditions. The removal efficiency of individual dyes in the mixture was calculated using the method described in Section S8. Fig. 6e–j showed that the mixed dyes could be effectively removed by 10 mg adsorbent within 10 min. Except for TB mixing with RdB, the removal efficiency of all dyes was above 90 %, demonstrating the rapid removal capacity of mixed dyes by Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites.

Considering the potential presence of suspended solids, trace metal elements, and chloride ions in industrial water, the adsorbent's performance should be investigated in real-time water samples. The tap water and untreated lake water (Qizhen Lake in Zhejiang University) were used to prepare TB and CR wastewater, and the relevant quality indexes of Qizhen Lake water were supplemented in Table S7. As depicted in Fig. 7 a and b, the adsorbent maintained good adsorption capacity for TB in tap water, while exhibiting a decreasing trend in lake water. For CR, the removal efficiency of CR was above 97 % in both lake water and tap water. Although TB was more susceptible to substances in the lake water than CR, it still achieved a removal efficiency of 88.3 %.

The adsorbent should be sealed and stored in a packaging bag during transportation or sales, and the packaging materials may affect the performance of the adsorbent in practical applications. Hence, the adsorbent was stored in a polyvinyl alcohol film (PVA) and a polyethylene film (PE), where PVA and PE have entirely different properties in terms of water solubility (Fig. 7c1-d2). The packaged adsorbent was added to TB, CR, ARS, and RdB solutions to explore such effects, and the ratio of adsorbent to packaging material was about 1:2. Compared with the unpackaged adsorbent, the adsorption capacity for TB was reduced by about 5 % by PVA packaging material (Fig. 7e), attributed to the weakening of hydrogen bonding between the dye and the adsorbent due to the –OH groups of PVA. Similarly, the removal efficiency of ARS and RdB showed a slight decrease under the influence of PVA. However, the removal efficiency of CR was seriously affected, dropping to 59.9 %, which proved that PVA is easier adsorbed to the surface of the adsorbent during the adsorption of CR. For PE materials, the residues of four dyes increased by 1 ∼ 3 %, mainly due to the small amount of adsorbent remaining in the PE bag that could not fully contact with the dyes.

The adsorbent may be stored for a certain period at room temperature or in packaging bags before use, which may affect the stability of the adsorbent. The adsorbent was added to TB and CR solutions after 30 days to understand the effect of storage time (Fig. 7f). The adsorbent still maintained excellent adsorption capacity for TB and CR under vacuum (4 °C). But this condition cannot be used for practical applications due to high cost. Significantly, after 30 days of contact with air at room temperature, the removal efficiencies of TB and CR were 94.4 % and 96.8 %, respectively, proving that the adsorbent has good storage stability. For the adsorbent sealed by PVA and PE, there was no obvious difference in the adsorption capacity before and after 30 days. Through the above analysis, PE and PVA can be used to slightly reduce adsorption capacity, thereby preventing contact between organic compounds and adsorbents, especially in sewage treatment plants or laboratories.

The performance of the adsorbent was evaluated under multiple desorption and re-adsorption cycles to investigate the potential for its recycling. A 0.1 M NaOH/ethanol mixture was used to desorb the dye, and the adsorbent was subsequently washed several times with ultrapure water to neutralize the pH [5]. Many OH− weaken the interaction between the dye and the adsorbent, resulting in dye desorption under alkaline conditions. The adsorbent showed acceptable adsorption capacity for TB and CR in up to five cycles (Fig. S13), and the removal efficiency for TB and CR decreased by about 43.2 % and 42.9 %, respectively. High pH was destructive to the crystal framework of UiO-66 MOF through the analysis in Section 3.3.2. FESEM, XPS, FTIR, and Zeta potential analysis were used to determine the effect of short time in a strong alkaline environment on the composites (Section S10.2). In addition to the pore filling mechanism, the collapse and degradation of the crystals were obvious even in a high pH solution for a short time. These results revealed that the adsorbent has good regeneration ability, which contributes to reducing the cost of wastewater treatment. Additionally, this experiment aims to provide valuable reference for subsequent research by further characterization of the recycled materials, such as using some mild desorption methods or avoiding the use of strong alkaline solutions.

XPS, FTIR, and AFM were used to explore the adsorption mechanisms for Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites after adsorption of TB and CR (Section S11.4). For the composites, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic attraction, π-π stacking, and pore filling were confirmed to exist between the adsorbent and the dyes (Fig. S20). Ce-UiO-66, Ce-UiO-66-F, Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 and Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 (Simple mixing) composites were used for the adsorption experiments of TB and CR to further explore the reaction mechanism (Section S11.5). The results revealed that hydrogen bonding is one of the direct factors contributing to the improvement of adsorption performance. Zeta potential, BET and FTIR were used to explore the contribution of hydrogen bonds to other adsorption mechanisms (Section S11.6). The hydrogen bonds of the composites helped facilitate the utilization of the pores, but had no obvious contribution to other adsorption mechanisms. In addition, since the purity of MOFs would not reach 100 %, the composites still retained a slight oxidation property in the strong acidic environments, which significantly enhances the absorption of CR (Section S11.7).

The adsorbent was compared with the previously reported adsorbents to understand the competitiveness of synthetic materials and shown in Table 3 . The qm of this adsorbent for TB is approximately 1.65 times that of Keratin/Ce-MOF and is the maximum value in the known reports. In particular, the qm of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4-2 composites for CR is about 2 and 5 times that of Ce-UiO-66 MOF before and after pH adjustment, respectively. Although the qm of the composites for CR and ARS is slightly lower than that of 2D Zn-MOF and [Ca(BDC) (H2O)3], respectively, the qm is still higher than that of most adsorbents within 10 min. Remarkably, the positively charged adsorbent shows a higher qm than that of most adsorbents for RdB with hydrogen bond donors. At the same time, the contact time of this adsorbent is shorter than that of most high-capacity adsorbents, indicating that wastewater remediation can be achieved in a short period. Significantly, storage stability as one of the critical factors for practical applications was explored for the first time from the perspective of storage time and packaging materials. Compared with wood-based activated carbon, the composites showed an average 5.6-fold increase in qm for the three organic dyes, while the potential cost increased by only 4 to 5 times (Section S12.1). The synthesis of the composites enhanced its adsorption capacity for dyes containing hydrogen bond donors, contributing to cost savings in wastewater treatment, such as laboratory wastewater discharge. Meanwhile, Tea-L-CDs and F had leakage problems, but the leakage concentration remained within an acceptable range (Section S12.2). In summary, the adsorbent not only has the characteristics of rapid adsorption and high capacity for multi-dye, but also has good storage stability and engineering application potential, indicating that the adsorbent has excellent competitiveness in wastewater purification.

MOF-based composites are typically developed to meet specific application requirements, including increasing specific surface area, altering surface charge, reducing costs, and enriching functional groups. Their overall performance is superior to that of individual MOFs. Some composites were created by immobilizing MOFs onto large molecular substances (graphene oxide, keratin, polycaprolactone, etc.) to enhance their physicochemical properties [4,62,75]. Simultaneously, small molecular substances (polyacrylic acid, polyethyleneimine, etc.) can be adsorbed onto the surface of MOFs to alter surface potential or introduce additional functional groups [37,76]. Compared to these two methods, the synthesis of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites focus more on developing its own active sites, followed by the introduction of external small molecular substances to enrich functional groups. Changes in the synthesis method also accompany variations in the crystal structure and specific surface area of MOFs. For example, the adsorption capacity or reusability of the synthesized material has been enhanced by altering solvents and adjusting salt solution concentrations [26,77]. In this study, the synthesis of Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites was carried out in an autoclave, which differs from the synthesis method used in previous studies on Ce-UiO-66 MOF [4,5]. The high-pressure conditions were employed to facilitate the reaction and crystal formation, thereby accelerating the synthesis of MOF [78]. MOFs are often synthesized or modified in a cost-effective and environmentally friendly manner, such as using low-cost chitosan or recycled waste [28,29]. In this work, Tea-L-CDs were incorporated with the aim of enhancing adsorption performance while reducing the production cost of the adsorbent. The emphasis is placed on improving adsorption capacity rather than simply reducing costs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of a system for automated dye filtration and residue detection
Rapid water purification using qualitative filter paper
ce:section.ce:para: The qualitative filter paper was selected to investigate the feasibility of blocking dye-attached Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites to achieve rapid water purification. Specifically, the absorbance of TB (100 mg/L), CR (200 mg/L), ARS (50 mg/L), and RdB (50 mg/L) solution was measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer before and after filtration, which can understand the contribution of filter paper to dye removal. After filtration, the dye concentration was calculated by UV–Vis spectroscopy and calibration curves, which were used to evaluate the removal effect of the filter paper on the adsorbent by comparing with the dye concentration after adsorption and centrifugation. Fig. 8 a–c and S25 showed that the filter device can quickly achieve water purification, and the details of adsorption and filtration were depicted in supplementary videos 1&2. From Table S12, the filter paper removed 0.73 % of TB and 1.93 % of RdB molecules. In comparison, the removal efficiency of CR and ARS was 20.1 % and 10.2 %, respectively, revealing that the adsorption capacity of the filter paper was affected by the type of dyes. The removal efficiency of dyes was not significantly affected after adsorption and filtration compared with adsorption and centrifugation. The filter paper in Fig. 8d was proved to achieve water purification by effectively blocking the dye-attached adsorbent, and the color change of the solution was shown in Fig. 8e and f.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility analysis of DCNNs for assessing contamination levels of TB and CR
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8g shows an automated detection device used to collect images of dye solutions for model training and then to rapidly assess TB and CR contamination levels (Levels A, B, C, D) using the trained model (Fig. S26). Levels A ∼ D correspond to the dye concentration (mg/L) ranges of [0, 0.1], (0.1, 1], (1, 5], and (5, 100), respectively. The classification accuracy of the four DCNNs was above 90 % (Table 4 ), indicating that TB and CR of different pollution levels can be effectively evaluated. MobileNetV3 performed best with 94.6 % training accuracy and 96.3 % validation accuracy, confirming that DCNNs have the potential to evaluate dye contamination for different water samples. Except for Mobileone, the total parameters were less than 10 million, and the number of floating-point operations (FLOPs) was between 0.2 billion and 0.7 billion. Thus, the time consumption of a single evaluation will be within 1 s. After the model was deployed to the CPU, the test accuracy of the four models for the newly prepared samples was more than or equal to 90 %, which proves the feasibility of the automated detection device (Supplementary video 3). About 5 % of the samples were misclassified during the model training process, mainly due to the sensitivity of the developed device being affected at low concentrations. In practical testing, the evaluation of water samples may be affected by the limited training samples, resulting in unstable prediction. Therefore, for the complex problems of different water samples and dyes, the model can be trained according to different water samples contaminated with different dyes, and then deployed on the cloud server to improve the classification accuracy. Meanwhile, the new samples can be used to optimize the parameters of the existing model to improve the stability of the application and save time continuously.

The automated dye filtration and contamination assessment system (source code: Section S15) was shown in Fig. 8i, which was mainly composed of valves, steering gear, Arduino single-chip microcomputer, mixing device, filter, automated detection device, and computer. The specific workflow is as follows: (1) the model stored in the cloud server was downloaded to the local computer, and the adsorbent was added to the dye-containing wastewater under constant stirring; (2) a countdown of about 500 s was displayed in the UI corresponding to the pretreatment of the wastewater. After stirring for 400 s, valve 1 was opened under the rotation of the steering gear, and the wastewater entered the automated detection device through the filter device; (3) valve 1 would automatically close when there was an appropriate amount of water into the filter device. After filtration, the level of water contamination was predicted and displayed in the UI; (4) according to the predicted results, one of the valves (Levels A ∼ D) would automatically open, and the suggestion of sewage discharge would be listed on the UI (Supplementary video 4). The adsorbent showed excellent potential for practical application by collocating cloud servers and this system, where the development of dye residue detection models is of practical significance for improving the efficiency of water contamination assessment. This system was proposed as an attempt of engineering application, hoping to improve the application potential of this study.

In this study, Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4 composites with strong hydrogen bonding was prepared to remove various dyes by modifying green CDs onto Ce-UiO-66-F4 MOF. The composites showed excellent universal adsorption capacity for dyes containing hydrogen bond donors. Through the exploration of the adsorption mechanisms, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic attraction, π-π stacking, and pore filling were identified as the main adsorption mechanisms, among which hydrogen bonding indirectly improved the utilization rate of pore filling. Under the strong acid environment, qm of the composites for CR was significantly improved, which was indirect enhancement of the adsorption mechanism by the effect of oxidation. Moreover, the adsorbent can maintain the stability of adsorption within 30 days, and the packaging materials had a negative impact on dye removal. Significantly, real-time water sample test and system test proved that the adsorbent has the potential for engineering application. Nevertheless, the composites reduced the specific surface area, leading to a decrease in the adsorption performance for dyes that do not contain hydrogen bond donors. In the future, the composites can be developed in the direction of increasing the universal adsorption range, which is of great significance for promoting practical applications. Overall, strengthening the hydrogen bonding of MOFs is a feasible choice to improve adsorption performance and achieve universal adsorption.

Maozhen Qu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.
